title: Whakairo Awa: Carving the River's Story / Te Whakairo i te Kōrero o te Awa
subject: Arts
culturalContext: Students will explore the art of whakairo (carving) through the story of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku (The Mangakōtukutuku Stream), a significant waterway to Ngāti Kahungunu that flows near our college. The stream's name references the mangakōtukutuku (blue duck/whio), a taonga species, and its patterns and flow inform traditional carving patterns. Students will connect with local carvers from the Ngāti Kahungunu iwi to understand how our awa informs artistic expression and cultural identity.
learningObjectives: Analyze and describe how the patterns and flow of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku can be interpreted into whakairo motifs.

Design and create a small-scale whakairo (e.g., a tokotoko or panel) that visually tells a story inspired by the awa, demonstrating understanding of traditional patterns like unaunahi (fish scales) and pūhoro (swift movement).
Present their artwork, articulating the specific cultural and environmental connections to Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku and its significance to Ngāti Kahungunu.
Collaboratively contribute to a class waka huia (treasure box) that digitally archives the stories behind each student's carving.

activities.title: He Haerenga: Journey to the Awa
activities.description: The class will visit Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku. Students will use sketchbooks to document the stream's flow, the textures of surrounding rocks and flora, and evidence of life (like the whio). They will record their observations and initial ideas for how these natural forms could translate into carving patterns.

activities.title: Te Kōrero me ngā Toi: The Story and the Art
activities.description: A local Ngāti Kahungunu carver (tohunga whakairo) will visit the classroom. They will share the pūrākau (story) of the awa, demonstrate basic whakairo techniques using soap or soft wood, and explain the meanings behind key motifs like the mangopare (hammerhead shark) which symbolizes strength and adaptability in water.

activities.title: Te Whakatakoto Mahere: Planning the Design
activities.description: Using their sketches and notes from the awa visit, students will draft a design for their carving. They must incorporate at least one traditional motif discussed by the carver and one original pattern inspired by their personal observation of the stream's environment. Designs will be peer-reviewed for cultural appropriateness and clarity of storytelling.

activities.title: Te Mahi Whakairo: The Carving Practice
activities.description: Students will transfer their final designs onto a piece of soap or a small, soft wood block (e.g., basswood). Using safe, appropriate carving tools, they will bring their designs to life, focusing on the depth, line, and texture that convey their story of the awa.

activities.title: Te Whakaaturanga: The Exhibition
activities.description: Students will write a short artist's statement explaining the story and cultural connections in their work. The class will hold a mini-exhibition for whānau and local experts. Each student will present their carving and statement, and contribute a digital photo and story to a shared class 'Digital Waka Huia' (e.g., a Padlet or Google Site).

resources: A local Ngāti Kahungunu carver (tohunga whakairo) as an expert guide
The book 'Ngā Toi: Māori Arts in Aotearoa New Zealand' by Julie Paama-Pengelly
Digital archive of whakairo patterns from Te Papa Tongarewa's online collection
Soft carving mediums (soap blocks, basswood), sketching materials, and safe carving tools (lino cutters, spoons)

tasks: Completed whakairo artwork demonstrating technical skill and incorporation of traditional and original motifs
Written or recorded artist's statement explaining the cultural and environmental narrative of the piece
Oral presentation of the work to the class and invited guests

nzcAlignment: Understanding the Arts in Context: Investigate the purposes of the arts in past and present cultures and contexts (UC)
Developing Practical Knowledge: Explore and use art-making conventions, applying knowledge of elements and selected principles through the use of materials and processes (PK)
Developing Ideas: Develop and refine ideas in selected art disciplines in response to a variety of motivations, using imagination and with reference to established models (DI)
Communicating and Interpreting: Describe and respond to the ways in which objects, images, and art-making processes communicate meaning (CI)
